# Transcrição: 04- Landing Page Design Example 

- **Fonte:** 04- Landing Page Design Example 
- **Duração:** 11:07
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Now, this example of landing page design, I want to give you an example of a landing page template that I created many years ago, which to this date as the time of recording is still in place for this company. So I've spent a lot of time understanding and studying user experience as part of my entry into the e-commerce world in 2005, came through conversion rate optimization, landing page analysis, Google Analytics, or sorry, analytics insights, A, B testing, all that kind of stuff. One of the things I really kind of try and work hard on is regarding the kind of real estate that you have. So real estate is when you're looking at a page, what is the land available? So the space available and what the message is that you're pushing out. One of the things that we did for this particular business was that we considered a mobile first design. So going back over a decade, as mobiles started to kind of not just generate more or have more traffic generated, but companies started to realize how important mobile design was. At that time, it wasn't really considered as the first approach. And even to today, most people or many people I see start with a design that's on the landing page, and then they fit it into the mobile experience. I like to look at it the other way around, which is let's start with a mobile experience first, and then let's switch it into a desktop experience. Because that way, you're thinking about the primary usage first for most businesses, which is mobile, and then adapting it for desktop. And that's kind of what we did here, but I'll show you the desktop design just to give you an idea of the element. So first of all, one of the things that I like to focus on is when you're landing on any landing page is orientate the user first. So the first thing I want to get across is, what is this page? What is this site? What's the proposition? Am I in the right place? So if I've just clicked an ad, and let's say this is the landing page, and the message says a better way to send money, and the ad's talking about transferring money abroad and sending money home, things like that, I immediately know that I'm in the right place. There's a complimentary image there that complements the message, and that's great. And then the call to action is right there. One of the things that I learned early on in my user experience journey was the difference between the type of users that land on any site. So you have what we call hunters, trackers, and explorers. Now hunters people that know exactly what they want, and they go straight for the kill. So they're hunting. Someone who's in the hunting mode could be someone that's come through a retargeting ad and landed on this page. So they know who this company is, they know what the product is, and now they're ready to transact, to purchase, to buy, whatever it is. For the hunter, we have this option here, which is to choose your country to send to and get started. So for the hunters, we have you.

## [03:04 - 06:10]

Then we talk about trackers. Trackers are more likely the people that are not ready to convert right now, but need a bit of convincing and they need a bit of orientation as well. The trackers are looking for signs of, or directions, they're looking like they're looking for the trials and footprints and directions and things like that. Then what we have developed here, what we did develop is looking at answering the main questions that they might have and navigating them into the right area based on what they're looking for. So for example, if you are looking to send money and you're interested in mobile money, okay, we've answered your query, and then you'll click here to learn more and you'll go into that page. And therefore the tracker has the ability to form their own path. So whereas the hunter wants to go straight for the kill, the trackers kind of looking through. Then you have the explorer. The explorer is the majority of your site visitor. So whatever site you're running, your conversion rate will generally never be a 100%. So that means that all the visitors you get coming to your landing page to your site, there'll be a small percentage that will purchase or become a lead, then kind of next level of users that are engaged and interested, but they've not signed up or purchased, then there's a bigger number that just do not give you any kind of lead data. They're your explorers. So for example, on a typical e-com store, you might have a conversion rate of let's say 2%, 3% for example, there you're a hunt, the majority of those are your hunters, people that want to buy and are ready to buy, there'll be some explorers in there and some trackers as well. Then you'll generally have 20% give or take, maybe even 30 that are possibly thinking about your product and their trackers, and you may or may not convert them, but that's where your retargeting comes in to turn a tracker into a hunter. Then the majority of people on your site, the 60, 70% of visitors you get, are explorers, they're window shoppers, they're just, they were bored, they wanted to click your ad, they wanted to see what you're about, maybe in three months time they'll turn into a customer, maybe even two days time, who knows, but right now they're not in buying mode, they're not really interested in looking further at your products, but they are just exploring. And because of that, I like to offer them routes through. So for the explorer, that's, they're generally the people that will go for the top navigation. So they just want to click around, etc, etc. Your goal is to always direct them back into your call to action. So if an explorer comes in, they want to click how it works, will give them the information, but ultimately will make it easier, easy for them to get back into our goal. So there's always a point back. The other point I want to raise about this kind of design is, we did some user research on understanding what people wanted to understand about the product and also the business when they came to this website. One of the biggest things that

## [06:10 - 09:10]

came out was brand awareness. So at the time, we raised, I think $40 million as part of a venture capitalist fund to play against some well known names. Because of that, people would land on the site and the biggest factor is trust. So if I'm sending money through your service, I want to know that I can trust you. So how can we bring that out? So we talk about why choose this business, for example, and we pulled out four of the biggest things that we found from research from emailing past customers and also responding to email inquiries and asking people that had put inquiries in to say, why are you not going forward? And kind of what would make you more confident in what we do? Then we kind of build the social proof here. So there's trust pilot reviews you can scan across, for example, and pulling out reviews that really relate to the type of people that we expect to be reading this as well. And then there's this block here, which is how to actually operate the service, which is a video and it plays and it gives you an idea of how simple it is and how fast it is. So for the visual learner, then we've got that covered off there. You could split test that and maybe run that up here as a video as well, which is something I would do as well. And then further down, we've got some kind of messages which try and push you into using this particular brand as opposed to competitors, for example, then we go into kind of the different options you've got to use the service. Then we've got some additional information on security. So security is obviously a big aspect for this particular brand. And then we've got the mobile app at the bottom. So all the way through, what we're doing is using the real estate to just focus on one thing at a time. So why choose this business? Why trust us? How does it work? Why choose you and not someone else? What are my options, for example? And I'm focusing on one thing at a time. If there's one thing that let's landing pages down is it's not a clear journey. And they're not really thinking about the mindset of the person on the page. Now, if you're starting off and you're just running ads and you've launched an e-com store or landing page and whatever you're doing, lead generation or sales, it may not be the first thing you do, but it has to be something you do before scaling to optimize your landing page. So this is an example of the thought process that goes in. Surveying customers to ask them, why did you choose this service? Who did you consider alongside us? Why did you choose us and not them? What I would do if you have the time and ability is to phone your customers up, speak to them, hear about how they talk about your service and what their journey was up until they visited your site because your data will only tell you once someone's clicked onto your ad what they're doing.

## [09:10 - 11:07]

So you look at analytics, for example, but what were they doing before they clicked your ad? Were they actually in the market for a product and your ad hit them and they clicked through? Those things can give you clues as to how to pitch your product, your messaging, how to create your landing page, for example, and it will help you to understand what the priority elements are for your product. So what if you're selling? Does it make sense to maybe not show the price at the top? So one of the previous examples we talked about bundling products, we showed the price right at the bottom after we sold in the value. I'll give you an example. I run a Facebook ads agency, I get people emailing in saying, interesting you running our ads, how much it cost. If I don't have the chance to convince you of the value first, and I just give you the cost, you're going to compare that to other agencies just purely based on cost. Therefore, I'm not going to be able to convince you to use our service, because not being the cheapest means we may lose out. So we do some discovery ahead of that. Same goes for your product page. If you're selling a $20 product, $30, $40, etc., you may not need to convince someone as much as if you're selling $100, $150, $200 plus product, then you need to build that no like and trust. And therefore your landing page needs to reflect that as well. So it's really important that when you choose a landing page experience, that it's considering the user journey, the three different types of users, hunters, trackers and explorers, and giving them all a route to ultimately purchase with you, that gives you higher conversion rates, potentially increases your average order value, because if you've brought them through, because you've really helped to solve a problem and you've convinced them, you've got a higher chance of upselling items, increases your lifetime value as well, because that brand affinity and the product affinity has increased as well. And it reduces your CPM because you've increased your engagement on the page. And therefore, it's a win-win-win across the board.
